text:
RESTAGING MULTIPHASIC CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS AND CT CHEST WITH IV CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 66-year-old female with borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head and uncinate process, stage III (T4N1M0 at diagnosis). Patient has completed 8 cycles of neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX; oxaliplatin dose-reduced to 68 mg/m2 from cycle 3 onward due to peripheral neuropathy). Restaging examination to assess treatment response and determine surgical candidacy. Baseline CA 19-9 at diagnosis 1,247 U/mL; most recently 85 U/mL. Prior restaging CT after 4 cycles demonstrated partial response with reduction of pancreatic head mass from 3.8 x 3.2 cm to 3.1 x 2.5 cm and decreased SMA contact.

TECHNIQUE: Multiphasic CT of the abdomen and pelvis performed using a pancreatic protocol with pre-contrast, pancreatic parenchymal phase (approximately 40 seconds), and portal venous phase (approximately 70 seconds) acquisitions following injection of 125 mL of iodinated IV contrast at 4 mL/second via right antecubital vein. Images reconstructed at 2.5 mm and 0.625 mm slice thickness in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. CT of the chest performed in the portal venous phase with 1.25 mm slice thickness reconstructed in axial and coronal planes. No oral contrast administered (pancreatic protocol). Multiplanar reformats reviewed.

COMPARISON: Restaging CT abdomen/pelvis obtained approximately 6 weeks ago (after 4 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX), and original diagnostic pancreatic protocol CT from approximately 5 months ago.

---

FINDINGS:

CT CHEST:

Lungs and airways: The lungs are clear. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidative opacities are identified. The airways are patent to the segmental level bilaterally. No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and hila: The mediastinum is unremarkable in contour. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. The thyroid gland is not fully imaged but the visualized inferior poles appear unremarkable.

Cardiac and pericardial structures: The cardiac silhouette is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The aorta and pulmonary vasculature appear normal in caliber.

Bony thorax: No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions in the imaged thoracic spine, ribs, or sternum.

---

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

Pancreas and primary tumor:
The previously identified hypoenhancing mass centered in the pancreatic head and uncinate process is again identified. On the current examination, the tumor measures approximately 2.6 x 2.1 cm in axial dimension (previously 3.1 x 2.5 cm at the 4-cycle restaging scan, and 3.8 x 3.2 cm at baseline), representing a further appreciable reduction in size over the course of neoadjuvant therapy. The mass remains poorly defined at its margins relative to the surrounding pancreatic parenchyma and adjacent soft tissues, with a persistent desmoplastic stromal reaction; however, the previously prominent hypodense perilesional halo appears somewhat less conspicuous compared with the baseline examination, suggesting an element of treatment effect within the surrounding stroma.

In the pancreatic parenchymal phase, the mass demonstrates minimal enhancement relative to the surrounding pancreatic parenchyma, consistent with the known desmoplastic adenocarcinoma histology.

Superior mesenteric artery (SMA): On careful multiplanar review, the tumor maintains soft tissue contact with the right lateral and posterior wall of the SMA, with the arc of contact now estimated at no more than approximately 80-90 degrees of circumference — a meaningful improvement compared with the mid-treatment scan and a substantial improvement from the greater-than-180-degree contact documented at baseline. There is no high-attenuation soft tissue stranding extending to encase the SMA adventitia over a wide arc, and the fat plane between the tumor and the vessel, while not fully re-established, appears partially reconstituted along the left-anterior surface of the artery. No SMA narrowing or luminal irregularity.

Superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and portal vein confluence: The SMV is now patent along its entire length with no appreciable luminal compromise. Residual soft tissue density is present at the site of prior SMV contact, but this appears to be at most mild superficial adherence rather than frank encasement. The lumen of the SMV appears round and fully patent on all phases. The portal-splenic-SMV confluence is widely patent without evidence of thrombosis or focal narrowing. Compared with the baseline scan, in which there was partial occlusion with luminal narrowing, the current appearance represents a substantial improvement.

Main pancreatic duct: Previously dilated at 6 mm at baseline and 4 mm at the mid-treatment scan, the main pancreatic duct upstream of the mass now measures approximately 3-4 mm in diameter, near the upper limit of normal. No intraductal calculi.

Pancreatic parenchyma: The pancreatic body and tail are atrophic in caliber, likely representing post-obstructive atrophy in the setting of the longstanding ductal obstruction. No new parenchymal masses or suspicious enhancement defects within the pancreatic body or tail.

Liver and biliary system: The liver is normal in size and attenuation. No focal hepatic masses, enhancing lesions, or hypodense nodules suspicious for metastatic deposits. The intrahepatic bile ducts are no longer dilated (previously there was mild intrahepatic biliary dilation at baseline). The common bile duct measures approximately 7 mm in diameter, improved from the 12 mm reported at baseline; no intraluminal filling defects. The gallbladder is present with a thin wall and no cholelithiasis. The cystic duct is unremarkable.

Lymph nodes: The previously noted peripancreatic/peri-aortic lymph node in the region of the superior mesenteric vessels, which measured 1.2 cm at baseline and 0.7 cm at mid-treatment, is now not clearly identified as a discrete enlarged node. In the current study, the largest visualized peripancreatic lymph node measures approximately 0.5 cm in short axis — well within the range of normal reactive or non-enlarged lymph nodes. No enlarged celiac axis, porta hepatis, retroperitoneal, or pelvic lymph nodes. No new nodal disease.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesions. The splenic vein is patent.

Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are slender and unremarkable. No adrenal nodules or masses.

Kidneys and ureters: Both kidneys enhance symmetrically and excrete contrast normally. No renal masses. No hydronephrosis. No nephrolithiasis. The ureters are not dilated.

Stomach and duodenum: The stomach and duodenum appear grossly unremarkable. The duodenum is intimately related to the tumor (as expected for a pancreatic head lesion) but no definite luminal compromise or obvious mucosal abnormality is identified on this CT. No gastric outlet obstruction.

Small and large bowel: The visualized loops of small bowel and colon are normal in caliber and wall thickness. No obstruction, perforation, or inflammatory change. No peritoneal soft tissue nodularity.

Peritoneum and mesentery: No peritoneal implants, omental caking, or ascites. The mesentery is not thickened. No free fluid.

Aorta and major vessels: The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber without aneurysmal dilation. The celiac axis, common hepatic artery, splenic artery, and left gastric artery appear patent. The SMA origin is patent. No vascular anomaly that would alter surgical approach.

Bony structures and soft tissues: No suspicious lytic or blastic osseous lesions in the visualized lumbar spine, pelvis, or proximal femora. No pathological fractures. The abdominal wall and subcutaneous soft tissues are unremarkable.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Continued progressive reduction in size of the pancreatic head/uncinate adenocarcinoma, now measuring approximately 2.6 x 2.1 cm compared with 3.1 x 2.5 cm at the 4-cycle restaging scan and 3.8 x 3.2 cm at baseline — reflecting an overall substantial decrease in tumor bulk over the full neoadjuvant course.

2. Meaningful improvement in the relationship of the tumor to the superior mesenteric artery: the arc of contact is now estimated at approximately 80-90 degrees or less of circumference, compared with greater than 180 degrees at baseline. While soft tissue still contacts the vessel, the degree of involvement is substantially diminished and may now approach a range more amenable to surgical resection.

3. The superior mesenteric vein now appears fully patent with only residual perivascular soft tissue at the prior site of contact; no luminal compromise is identified. The portal-SMV confluence is widely patent.

4. Interval resolution of the previously noted enlarged peripancreatic lymph node; the largest visible peripancreatic node now measures 0.5 cm and is not suspicious for active nodal disease.

5. Interval improvement in biliary dilation: the common bile duct and intrahepatic ducts are no longer dilated, suggesting improved biliary drainage.

6. No hepatic metastases. No pulmonary nodules or thoracic disease. No peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites.

7. Taken together, the imaging findings reflect a favorable and ongoing treatment response to neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX. The vascular anatomy, while still showing residual tumor-vessel contact at the SMA, has evolved in a direction that may support surgical candidacy. Multidisciplinary reassessment for resectability is appropriate; consideration of further local consolidation prior to definitive surgery may be warranted given the persistence of SMA contact.

Reporting radiologist: [Attending Abdominal Radiologist]
Electronic signature applied.
